title: Wax polishing a period piece of furniture
subtitle: Build a protective wax finish on bare or previously waxed timber
excerpt: How to apply a wax polish to antique or period furniture: cleaning the surface, applying beeswax or carnauba wax in thin coats, allowing each coat to harden before buffing, and understanding the difference between a freshly waxed surface and a fully built finish.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics. The Mechanic's Workshop Companion. Collins Complete DIY Manual.

glossaryTerms.definition: A blend of beeswax and turpentine or mineral spirits, sometimes with added carnauba for hardness. Applied in thin coats and buffed to a soft sheen. Feeds and protects bare or stripped timber without forming a brittle film; will re-dissolve if solvent contacts the surface.

Vigorous buffing of a waxed surface with a soft cloth or brush that generates friction heat, melting and compressing the wax film into a harder, more durable layer. A burnished wax surface is distinctly harder and more water-resistant than an unburnished one.

content.text: A wax finish is the traditional protective surface for period timber furniture. Unlike lacquer or varnish, it can be repaired and renewed without stripping.
takes five to seven thin coats to build a proper film. One thick coat buffed immediately gives only surface sheen, not the depth of a built finish.

Wipe the entire surface with a cloth lightly dampened with methylated spirit to remove old polish residue, silicone sprays, and accumulated grime. The cloth should lift a brown or grey residue on the first few passes; work until the cloth comes away clean. Allow the surface to dry for one hour before applying any wax. On bare or newly stripped wood, lightly sand with 240 grit on the sanding block before cleaning.
Step 2, apply the first wax coats

Apply a thin smear of beeswax polish with a lint-free cloth or a small piece of 0000 steel wool, working along the grain. The coat should be thin enough that the grain of the wood is still visible through it. Allow to harden for a minimum of 30 minutes in a warm room; the wax should feel dry and slightly chalky to the touch before buffing. Buff with a clean cloth using firm circular motions, then finish with straight strokes along the grain. Apply three coats on the first session.
Step 3, build and burnish the finish
Apply two further coats over the following two days, allowing each to harden fully. For the final coat, apply the wax sparingly and after 20 minutes

vigorously with a soft cloth, generating warmth through friction. The surface will develop a deeper, harder sheen. A well-built wax finish repels water beads and has a depth that a single-coat application never achieves.

Once the finish is built, maintenance is a single thin coat once or twice a year and a buff. Any scratches or localised dulling can be addressed with a small amount of polish on a cloth, buffed out, without stripping or starting again. This is the main advantage of wax over varnish: the finish remains repairable for the life of the piece.
